stories.title: Rise of the Planet of the Tradwives
stories.description: A wine mom, a total jerk, a child in a puffin suit, and an exceptionally attractive space-alien. Worst dinner party in the galaxy.
stories.content:
If you live under a rock, here’s a lesson on world history: a few years back, humankind had a planet-wide sex war. People who are attracted to men and their allies, versus….

Well. Men. 

Broad strokes, it was a mess. Birthrates plummeted. Dating apps went tits up. People left their husbands in droves, and the resulting chaos devolved into infighting. Gay men went voluntarily celibate; bisexual men tried to convince the women in their swiftly-evaporating dating pool that their fifty-percent gayness made them “not that bad”; straight women ignored the bisexual men and made a bunch of platonic communes together; bisexual women ignored the bisexual men and the straight women and flung themselves at the mercy of the lesbians; the lesbians tried to close ranks but couldn’t because the bisexuals wouldn’t quit pretending to be lesbians, and anyone on the ace or nonbinary spectrums not personally identifying as members of the aforementioned groups began exclusively dating one another on the grounds that the rest of us had seriously fucking lost it this time. 

For context, I am a bisexual woman. 

No, I have never pretended to be a lesbian.

Yes, I am one of the people who left their husbands during this trying period of socioeconomic upheaval.

No, I do not regret doing so.

*However*.

Divorce comes with some downsides. Namely, my child’s expectation that I answer the following question:

“Mommy, is Daddy bringing his sex-doll?”

I choke on the Chardonnay I’ve been guzzling while pretending to do dishes. 

In choking, I drop the glass. 

It breaks. 

“Fuck,” I say. “You heard that, Harbor?”

I named him after this place. Bar Harbor, as in, the location of our idyllic cliffside beach house in Acadia. As in, *that’s how much I fucking love it here, okay, I named a whole child after it, and now fucking Kevin and his fucking sex doll are crashing my weekend, the fucking bogarts.* 

Waddling delicately from the mess, Harbor frowns at me from beneath the bill of his Atlantic Puffin onesie as he sticks out a wing and points to the swear jar. Obediently, I feed it a twenty on my way to the broom closet. 

I know I shouldn’t call my ex-husband’s new wife a sex doll, and especially not earshot of my immature spawn. Harbor’s got enough external forces working to turn him into a misogynist without his own mother giving him a Madonna/whore complex. In about a year, terms like sex doll will probably be considered slurs, and I will probably feel really, really bad I ever used them. 

It’s just. 

It’s hard not to use derogatory language when referring to a population that has single-handedly empowered the mediocre white man to fail upwards *again*. Like. They could have helped us stomp the incels into the ground, and what did they do? They *helped the fucking incels*, oh my God, thinking about it makes me want to gouge Sex Doll’s eyes out with the broken-off stem of my wine glass. 

But probably, I should stop calling her a sex doll. Probably, she’s got lots of skills that have nothing to do with her holes, like taking pictures with cows and collecting baskets of eggs and pretending to beekeep and looking weirdly good in tablecloth dresses and baking allergen-free cookies and not accidentally breaking wine glasses or cursing in front of children and, likely, judging people who break wine glasses and curse in front of children. 

By the time I’m finished cleaning up all the chard and shards, the abandoned breakfast dishes in the sink are rattling with vibrations from my ex-husband’s Porsche. Harbor claps his wings over his ears, and oh my God, Kevin, it isn’t like you don’t own a perfectly regular-sounding suburban. How hard is it to remember your kid has auditory sensitivities? 

But then, in order to do that, he’d need to think about someone other than himself, so. Perhaps I ask too much. 

Kevin cuts the engine and exits the sports car before strolling around to the passenger’s side, and I admit it. When he opens the door for her, my upper lip curls.

The shimmering scales of a lavender-colored ankle glint from beneath the open passenger door, then disappear into a lace-embroidered kitten heel that plants itself on the cobblestone driveway. She stands, waits while Kevin shuts the door behind her, then primly takes his offered arm before tottering towards the house. Her dress is corseted, and while it does have some tablecloth-y florals on it, it isn’t wide-skirted at the bottom like I expected. It’s bodycon, hugging generous curves, and I am not kidding when I tell you, my mouth literally waters as I rise to the tip-toes of my sock feet and fumble around in the cupboard for a new wine glass. I will definitely need to get sloshed if I am going to endure two days in the same house with *that*.

I mean. 

With *her*. 

A sentient being. A…person. Essentially. Not just a bite-your-knuckle-and-moan piece of exoskeleton—

Um.

Back to the sex war. Did I forget to mention the part where we got invaded by aliens? 

Shoot. I keep doing that.

stories.title: Burn Book
stories.content:
Local legend has it that when the devil went down to Georgia, he settled in the mountain town of Purity. 

From there, he founded the largest megachurch the Appalachian south had ever seen, sponsored innumerable Despicable Things including mission trips, fake clinics, and gambits for office by racists, robbed state and country of taxes annually, and fathered seven children before he kicked the bucket.

One legitimate son, doubtlessly named to reap the benefits of his father’s lucrative sins. 

Six bastard daughters. Doubtlessly, notnamed to reap shit. 

On a witch-tits morning in January, I huddle shotgun in a F-150 Raptor belonging to the son.

“You lying motherfucker.” 

Beside me, Noah’s hands tighten on the wheel before he cuts the engine, circles around, and opens my door.

“Get out of the truck, Lily.” 

I do not get out of the truck. Instead, playing with the scarf in my lap, I debate the merits of strangling him with it.

“You said they wouldn’t be here.”

The hot pink school bus blocking the driveway says different. 

“Did I say that?” Noah peers skyward. “I seem to remember saying I hadn’t seen them, and I haven’t. Because they did not attend the settlement of the estate, because they are *holding my house hostage*.”

Past him, the property looms. An eyesore of a macmansion, perched on the highest peak of Fallen mountain. 

“Do you consider it even slightly relevant that your father built this place with money he owed Matilda Wilde in child support?” 

Noah snaps his fingers and points. “See, this is why I need you. Mercenary thinking.”

 “*Mercenary*?”

 “You’re the only one who can make her see sense.”

No need to clarify which *her* he means.

“I’m not going in there.”

“Aw, come on. What do you think they’re going to do to you?”

I eyeball the snow-covered terrain. “Some kind of Yellow Jackets situation, likely.”

“Don’t be ridiculous,” he snaps. “My sisters have never eaten anyone.”

Note, he does not say, my sisters have never ritually sacrificed anyone.

I suspect this is because he can’t.

I fix him with my most lascivious grin. “As I recall, one of your sisters used to eat *me* for—”

Like Fallen House heard us, the front door bangs open and spits out my ex-girlfriend. 

I throw myself out of the vehicle and shut the door with my ass. Adopting an expression of boredom, I start to examine my nails before I realize I can’t, I’m wearing gloves. 

As she sails toward us, I can’t stop myself from drinking her in. 

Rangy build, massive facial features. Eyes their own planets, cheekbones like switchblades. Bloodred hair, spilling to her ass. 

Ironically, although her father refused to acknowledge her, Seraphina Wilde inherited a lot more from Gabriel Morningstar than the son he bothered to claim. Ancient, elemental magic.

Steel in her veins. 

Fire on her tongue.

Ice in her chest. 

Madness in her eyes.

Her dress billows around in the wind as she moves. Tents, then presses flat to her body in a constantly-moving patchwork that renders it impossible to imagine what she looks like under there, but I don’t need my imagination.But I don’t need my imagination any more than I need her silhouette wiggling at me from inside a tight dress. 

I remember the inches of her. The handprints. 

Every time I see her now, my traitorous heart flings itself against my chest in desperation to reach hers, not understanding that it can’t. 

She doesn’t have one. 

At my side, Noah makes a sound in the back of the throat that sounds impressively like concern. “Is she not wearing any…?”

“Shoes?” I finish lightly. “No, she isn’t.”

Incidentally, every time I see her now, she also looks a little more deranged. Now that she’s closer, I can see that her dress is not only very large and very white, but very, *very* covered in lace. 

Not cottagecore lace. Haunted doll lace. The kind you see in the movies and implicitly understand will be strewn with vivisected organs by the end. Hem licks her ankles, bare feet sinking into the snow with her steps.

Seraphina falls to a halt, too close to me.

“Sorry to hear about your father,” I tell her, not very sorrowfully. 

“Not enough to attend the funeral, I noticed,” she returns. No malice in it. We both know she only attended the funeral to desecrate his grave.

Silence grates between us.

“Well—look.” Noah throws an arm in my direction. “I brought her, so. Y’all need to leave.”

As the demand registers, I bark out a laugh. “You brought me here to *barter*?”

Two sets of apple-green eyes blink at me, like the immorality of treating people like livestock is too esoteric to grasp. 

For a pair of sociopaths, it probably is.

Noah recovers first. “Look. It’s not my fault, okay? Seraphina–she said she needed to talk to you, and she’s kind of holding all the cards right now—”

I snort, because please. Seraphina Wilde, holding all the cards *right now*? Try always.

“Try always,” Seraphina demures, and I stiffen. 

She does that a lot. Plucks thoughts from my head like wildflowers. Used to make me feel seen, special. 

Now, I know it’s a party trick she can do with anyone.

“Cut the shit. What could you possibly have to say to me that you haven’t already?” 

Seraphina’s face goes inexplicably soft. “I’m sorry.”

Breath spits between my teeth. “Fuck you.”

“Baby, I know you’re angry with me,” Seraphina croons, like she’s talking to an injured animal. “I admit, I’ve…checked up on you, shall we say, over the years.” Her lashes sweep low over her cheeks. “You’ve built a formidable reputation. Cross my heart, what I’m about to ask of you is strictly…professional.” 

From the voluminous folds of her skirts, she produces…huh. A bible. 

Noah frowns. “Where’d you get that?” 

“Your room,” Seraphina says absently, flipping it open. 

“‘Cause it’s mine. He left it to me.”

“Do you know why?”

Snow dusts the tops of Noah’s boots as he shuffles around. “I guess, he left everything to me. So.” 

Seraphina smiles beatifically. “No. I mean, do you understand its value?”

“I don’t… it’s an heirloom, okay, he couldn’t even read it. Print’s too faded.”

A pious lilt enters her voice. “Gabriel Morningstar couldn’t read it because it was never his. The most closely-guarded possession in his miserable life, and it belongs to me.” 

Noah's face turns puce. He reaches for the book, but Seraphina parries easily. Turns one page, then another. 

Her fingers still, eyes growing unfocused as some opaque emotion rushes across her face before her eyes snap to mine.

“Lily. Come see.”

My stomach drops.

If I get any closer, something catastrophic might happen.

Like, I might accidentally smell her. 

Or her hair might start blowing around and smack me in the face like it used to. Or I might accidentally brush my arm against her tits, I mean, they’re like rockets right now under that doily she’s wearing—

“Uh.” I manage. Intelligently. “I’m good.”

Seraphina just stares. Maddeningly sure of my obedience. 

When I don't move, she gives a regretful little sigh. Closes the distance between us, and I do. I smell her.

Seraphina smells like your richest friend’s mom. Stupid-expensive candles, a bakery’s dessert in the oven.  Deceptively sweet.

Unlike other senses, smell goes directly to the brain’s limbic system. Which means that even when she’s staring at you with frightening, barely-leashed hunger in her eyes, you feel safe. You want to trust her.

Fuck. 

Schooling my expression, I try to focus. 

At first, the pages are incomprehensible.

But then, names rise. Glittering, crimson ink, bubbling up like blood on the sliver of a new wound. 

Some, I don’t recognise.

Others, the not-so-good people of Covenant Mountain Fellowship, signing away their souls to the everlasting mansion in glory. 
	The sheriff. The mayor. The high school football coach. And others, three in particular, tucked almost past notice in the bottom left corner. 

*Ryan French. Fielding Eastwood. Jacob Newport.*

I get it now. Not a bible. A ledger. 

Involuntarily, I shoot a glance past her towards Harlot House. Sure enough, five faces watch us through windows. 
I ran with them, rode for them, for so long. A hanger-on who held tighter and more desperately than the rest. White knuckles. Blistered palms. Poured-out like flat soda off the Blue Ridge Parkway the moment Seraphina lost her taste for me. 

It should hurt, seeing them. But I bite my lip on a grin.

Nosy witches. 

One of them spots me looking and waves. Beatrice has always been the friendlier, more enthusiastic of the two, but—no. That’s Lucy. The twins both write with their left hands, but Lucy’s right-handed in every other respect.

The thought floats that it’s been, God, half a decade, and I still remember that. Still tell the two apart so easily, I know which one waves at me from across a lawn and inside a house. 

Matilda Wilde gave birth to Lucy and Beatrice Wilde in a blow-up pool between our two trailers under a full pink moon. After Matilda and Seraphina, I was the first person to hold them, and I saw them nearly every day until they were teenagers. I taught them how to write their names, ride a bike, apply mascara, catch a fish, shoot a three-pointer, hit a homer, insert a tampon, kill a deer, throw a punch, shop for jeans, shoplift candy bars, cut their hair, train a dog, subtract fractions, and bake a cake using roughly five different substitutes for eggs. 

Seraphina and I referred to them as ‘the babies’ until they were about six years old. Playing family.

When the twins were eighteen, three of their classmates–Ryan French, Fielding Eastwood, and Jacob Newport–put Beatrice Wilde in the ICU. 

On prom night.

Glancing again at those familiar, oft-quoted names, I can’t pretend I wouldn’t cut my teeth on it. These fuckers, begging. Desperate screams, dawning horror when they realize their money, friends, and lies can’t save them now that the devil has come to collect. 

I mean, shit. Figure out how, I’ll drag them to hell twice. 

I fold my arms. Squeeze my biceps so tight, the circulation cuts. “I don’t work for you anymore,” I tell her quietly, and Seraphina lifts a shoulder as if to say, eh, not yet you don’t*.* 

“I considered finding someone else. There are other…what do you call yourself? A demon-slayer, monster-hunter...? But you know as well I do, the hardest monsters to vanquish are human. I needed the best, and….” she trails off with another little shrug, and her mouth curves. “What can I say? You’re the best I’ve ever had.”

Noah groans.  “Seraphina, don’t be gross. You’re my sister.”

“Shut up, Noah.”

We say it in unison, neither of us breaking eye contact until I force mine shut.

Instruct myself to hate her. 

But the moment I open them, my heart does the flinging thing. Already, it feels too-big in my chest. Fissures, climbing higher up glass walls. 

This is all it takes. 

Triumph and fire erupt in Seraphina’s eyes. Her nails lengthen into claws around the book as her features distort, serrating until she’s more predator than person. 

The bifurcated end of her tongue flicks between her lips on a serpentine hiss. 

“*Let’s make a deal*.”

Not a request. Not even a command. 

An incantation. One that compels me to agree with brutish, knee-buckling force. 

But I only grin. 

Local legend has it that when the devil went down to Georgia, he settled in the mountain town of Purity. 

Not true. She couldn’t leave fast enough.

Now, look. I’m nobody special. I’ve soaked my pillow with tears over her exactly as many times as one might expect from a heartsick, small-town, queer-as-folk cliche, and that’s—fine, whatever, I’m working it out with my therapist. 

But here’s the thing about me. When Seraphina Wilde allows the part of herself that passes for a soul to recede, yielding to her worst until she looks less a human woman and more like an unfuckable hell-crawler, I don’t piss myself like most people. 

If anything, it’s a reprieve. 

Seraphina Wilde kills me. But the Devil?

Her, I can handle. 

Turns out, it’s pretty hard to drown in the pools of someone’s eyes when they’ve got eternal flames shooting out at you from the blacks.

For the first time since she appeared, I feel as though she’s lifted her heel from my throat. My heart stops shuddering behind my ribs; my lungs breathe easy, and when I speak, my voice pours out Tennessee-whiskey smooth. 

“Damn, Finn. It *has* been a long time. You used to know better than to try that brimstone shit with me.”

Seraphina’s face goes back to normal, the flames in her eyes doused as she claps the book closed. Two rosy splotches appear on her cheeks.

I swear, I could sing.

“We both know I’ll end up doing whatever you want, so I’ll save us both some time, have Misty dust off our old contract and fax it over.” 

 On my way past Noah, I swipe the keys. He sputters, then hurries over and flings himself into the passenger’s seat, growling, “*I can’t believe you would leave me with them.*”

The audacity. 

I pull a three-point turn, gratified when Seraphina is forced to step out of my path. Before I leave, I roll down the window. 

“Head’s up. I’ve gotten a *lot* more expensive since you last put me on retainer.  Soul’s still off the table, though.”

stories.title: Blood and Hysteria
stories.description: Currently Unavailable: Soon to be published in Scylla’s “Who Am I?” Anthology
stories.content: Currently Unavailable: Soon to be published in Scylla's "Who Am I?" Anthology

stories.title: Mr Redmond’s Dietary Program for Scientifically-minded Young Ladies
stories.description: A girl-gang of radioactive zombies goes skinny-dipping.
stories.content:
*1955*

*Waltham, Massachusetts*

“Saint.” 

Her name, whispered in the dark by the voice that had given her nightmares for as long as she’d retained the ability to dream. 

Saint’s eyes flew open. With one of its last remaining beats, her heart leapt into her throat. 

*Canary*.

In all her glory. Standing over her bed like a hawkish, frizzy-haired angel. 

Not a dream. A hallucination, perhaps, or hey, maybe hell. Maybe she’d finally gotten lucky and died, thank fuck.

It sucked. Being the last to go. 

Saint knew better than to try Canary’s name aloud. Too many consonants. But she tried mouthing it before she remembered no one had ever actually called her that to her face. She received the appellation post-mortem, once Saint and the others figured it out. 

“*Don’teatitdon’teatitdon’teatitdon’teatitdon’teatitdon’t—”*

On their first day in Mr. Redmond’s Program for Scientifically-Inclined Ladies, Canary had taken one single bite of her nutrition bar before flipping the breakfast table. On a banshee’s endless wail, she’d delivered the warning until Mr. Redmond grabbed her by the throat and  shoved her into the arms of Nurse Watts, growling, “Shut her up.”

Canary had sensed the poison when none of the rest of them could, and her sensitivity had killed her. 

A canary. 

None of them saw Canary again, but they remembered her. Talked about her. Whenever one of them was feeling maudlin or weepy, they’d talk. 

Should have listened. 

Forgetting herself in an embarrassment of joy, Saint tried to lift her head. 

The room spun as she fell into a coughing fit. 

“Aw, shit, I’m sorry. Shhh, shhh. Don’t try to move, okay?”

The cot dipped as Canary sank down beside her. With delicate lightness, as though Saint’s skull were a soap bubble held in her hands, she lifted Saint’s head and rested it on her legs. 

The room spun anew.

Saint found she didn’t mind so much. 

“Guess who I found?”

Saint squinted as Canary balanced a series of pinup photos on the raking bones of her chest. 

When Saint recognised the woman in the photos, she sucked in a breath. 

Another coughing fit. 

Cordelia.

Well. Blade, now, on account of how she’d stabbed them all in their backs. Hot contender for the number-one spot on the kill lists. 

Take the opportunity, Cordelia had urged, after Mr. Redmond completed his presentation on the Program for Scientifically-Inclined Ladies. Saint was so *smart*, so *detail-oriented*, so *patient*, so *sensible*, she’d make an *excellent* scientist. 

She should do it. Better herself. Maybe someday, she’d be someone. 

After Saint signed up, others followed. Everybody trusted Saint’s judgement. 

They shouldn’t have. Saint had stars in her eyes like crazy for Cordelia. 

After setting up the program with the nurses and the tech students, Mr. Redmond had left. Cordelia, newly-hired as his personal secretary, left too. 

After Saint sprayed them liberally with blood, Canary took the photos back. 

“Back when it was just me, I had some time on my hands. I wanted to find anyone who had anything to do with the program,” she said. “Blade doesn’t work for Redmond. She robbed him and ran off with his daughter not long after she left Ward 5. Now she’s a dancer in New York City, and check it out. She’s fucking famous. Steam you at all? ‘Cause it does me. Saw red for a week when I found out. Money, influence, and she hasn’t lifted a finger for us.”

Saint considered, seriously, using all the breath left in her on a scream. 

“She left you here. Drinks champagne while you choke on blood.”

Saint hadn’t known herself capable of this, this feeling. By the time she’d suffered enough that she might otherwise have succumbed to fantasies of rage and revenge, she felt nothing but pain, grief, and exhaustion. Fastasized about nothing but a gulp of fresh air, a meal that wouldn’t make her sick, an hour of deep sleep. 

Slugger, who had decked everyone in Ward 5 at least once by the time she died, had been especially concerned by Saint’s positive attitude. Anger, she insisted, would keep her alive. 

The irony. After Canary, Slugger died first.

Carefully, Canary repositioned Saint’s head on the pillow and slipped off the cot. In the movement, Saint’s blanket slipped and revealed her arm. 

Saint fastened her gaze to the ceiling. Refused to look at Canary’s face. 

Fuck pity. At this point, honestly, fuck care and tenderness. She’d lost count of how long she’d been rotting in Ward 5 alone. Lightbulb and Hound had taken their last rattling breaths on the same night, the traitors. 

She refused to look at Canary’s face, but she heard the breath catch in her throat. Saw, from the corner of her eye, Canary’s fingers hover uncertainly over the burns and bruises as though to soothe.

Goosebumps rose on her skin, and the blanket returned. Canary crouched down, voice low and urgent in her ear.

“Listen to me. No matter what you decide you want, we won’t leave. You can’t see us, but we’re outside, and we’ll stay until the end. But if you die without fire in your belly, I can’t promise She’ll let you cross.”

Fire. In her belly. 

Kid stuff. 

Saint wanted to burst into flames. She wanted embers raining from the sky. She wanted to hunt Cordelia down like a rabbit and incinerate her where she stood. 

Perhaps not so incapable of revenge-fantasizing after all. 

Slugger would have approved. 

Her voice made a sort of whistling sound when she tried to use it. Tasted metal in her throat.

“*Who’s ‘She’*?”

More coughing. More blood. 

Canary rested a hand on Saint’s chest, pressed a cup of water to her lips. As she stood, strawberry hair whispered over Saint’s cheek. 

She smelled clean. Soap and flowers. 

Saint had nearly forgotten what all of it felt like. 

Soap. Flowers. 

Hair. 

As she slipped out, she paused. “Remember we’re here, okay? On the grounds. Right outside.”

Minutes after she left, Nurse Pound arrived with breakfast. 

Oatmeal. Radioactive materials mixed in with the raisins. 

Saint spent the day stoking the flames, and it leached her of strength completely. 

…

The night Saint died, a thunderstorm struck, or so she guessed. Lucidity teased her. Dancing close, then twirling away. 

In her mind’s eye or in reality, she saw lots of flashing lights. Heard screams.

Fire. 

Nurse Watts and Nurse Pound burst into her room, damn-near broke Saint’s matchstick bones taking vitals. Rubbed goopy, stinging junk into her sores, drew blood in their rough handling of her. Poured water down her throat until she choked, and the water ran from her mouth onto the pillow. When they ran out of ministrations, they sobbed for mercy on the cracked-tile floor. Her deathbed, an altar. 

The door creaked. Light, when it fell on her, broke open her head like an axe. Saint, vision swimming, shut her eyes. But a person, or maybe lots of people, must have entered. An avalanche of footsteps.

Someone hissed. 

From where they huddled by her bed, Nurse Watts and Nurse Pound began to scream.

A very, very bright light glowed through Saint’s lids. 

Silence fell. 

Saint heard a sizzle, smelled burning. Death vibrated against her lips when she tried to breathe, and nothing. 

…

“Are you kidding me?” Hound’s voice.  

Saint’s stomach flipped. For once, not with nausea. 

With *happiness.* 

“You’ve only been in bed for a year. Get *up*.”

*Schlop.*

Something hit her face. Slid down her cheek and into her lap as she sat up. Clumps of mud fell from her lashes as she blinked and found mud covering every square inch of her. 

Not that it mattered so much, really. Not if Hound was there.

She squeezed her eyes shut and dug her nails into the earth, afraid to think it. Afraid to ask, but she asked anyway.

“Where am I?” 

“Woodfinch Cemetery,” came someone else’s succinct reply. 

Lightbulb.

Saint’s stomach gave another lurch as her heart beat faster. 

“Unmarked grave, in your case. A nonnegotiable part of the crossing, I’m afraid, but it’s over now. You did it. Congrats.”

Prompt. Brisk. No-nonsense.

Lightbulb. *Lightbulb.*

“Smidgen of an update, the institute burned down. No survivors. Tragic*.*” 

Slugger.

Tears sprang to Saint’s eyes. 

Holy shit. Holy shit. Holy shit. 

Steeling herself, she looked up and found Slugger hanging over the edge of the hole where Saint huddled. Taking Slugger’s outstretched hand, Saint eased to her feet on pillowed joints. Slugger’s head disappeared as Saint reached up, flattened her palms against the grass, and pushed. Her muscles hummed, bearing her weight effortlessly as she rose onto level ground. 

Bathed in the glow of fireflies and an egg-yolk moon, she saw them all. 

Hound. 

Lightbulb. 

Slugger. 

Canary.

Smith. 

Eve. 

Jekyll. 

Hollywood. 

Not trembling or bed-bound. Not blanketed in threadbare cotton, not fighting for breath or consciousness or speech, but *there.* Strong-limbed, wicked-grinned, clad in denim and leathers and gel because Slugger, Smith, and Eve had hair growing back. 

All over, their veins glowed bright green beneath the skin. Their eyes, too, in the irises. A little darker. Like emeralds.

Hound toed the earth with her boot. Held out a hand, as though examining her nails. “Yeah, so. Turns out MIT and Nutrí-yum were onto something with the radioactive oatmeal after all.” 

Saint watched, fascinated, as the glowing green lines began writhing along Hound’s fingers like snakes.

“Pretty cool, right?” Canary spread out her arms, showing off how the luminescent ropes undulated on her biceps when she flexed. 

“You look like comic book characters.” 

“Yeah?” Smith strolled closer, bent a finger over her mouth. A curl tumbled down the center of her forehead as she studied Saint with a critical eye. “Well, you look like the swamp thing.”  She dug around in the bag at her hip and produced a towel, jeans, and a t-shirt. “They’ll work. If not, I’ve got the jeans in one other size and the tee in two. Go ahead. Take a dip.”

Saint followed Smith’s gaze and saw, past the gravestones and the train tracks, the Charles river glinting black ink in the dark.

“You’re still wearing the gown under the mud, you know,” Hollywood added, the innocence of her voice belied by the wicked cut of her grin. “You could take it off, burn it. Try out the green. Think of it as your initiation.” She wiggled her fingers, and the glowworms danced. 

Saint rolled her eyes. Stripped. Tossed the gown onto the grass. 

“Hold out your hand, palm out, towards the gown. Like this.” As Jeckyll demonstrated, Saint copied.

Easy enough.

“Now think about how you never, ever, ever want to wear it, look at it, or think about it ever, ever again,” Canary sing-songed, bouncing on her toes as she wove between the others. 

Saint concentrated on the feeling. Let the revulsion and bitterness well up in her. 

Sent it out. 

A clean line, the same glowing green as the veins, shot from her palm. When it met the gown, it bled into the fabric, dissolving it, leaving nothing.

“Scorched earth, baby.” Slugger pumped a fist.  

Beside her, Canary stopped weaving and began shuffling backwards down the hill. Cartwheeling her arms, she cried, “Oh, no-oh, I’m falling…I think I might fall all the way into the river!”

Smith grabbed her sleeve. “You are wearing a Schotts Perfecto motorcycle jacket, you wasteful, destructive ASSHOLE—“

Canary twisted out of it, leaving the jacket hanging on Smith’s hand. 

As Canary peeled off her tank top and shucked off her jeans, Slugger, Eve, Jeckyll, and Hollywood followed suit. Smith huffed at their backs as five naked women leapt over the train tracks and splashed into the river, screaming from the cold. 

“No shit it’s cold in fucking October,” she muttered, bending to drape Canary’s leather over the tombstone where she’d left her bag. “Huh. Weird. Strange. Peculiar.” She paused, making a show of examining its contents before she straightened. “How on *earth* did this get in here? Truly, I cannot imagine.” 

Her eyes sparkled as she presented the bottle of J&B Rare. 

Hound grabbed it. Took a slug.

Removed her clothes.

*Slowly*.

Shimmying her hips. Waggling her eyebrows. 

At Lightbulb.

“Fine. *Fine*,” Lightbulb muttered, slinging her jacket onto a gravestone as Smith and Hound shot off after the others, already passing the bottle between them. “Not like we have work to do or anything. Not like we have a hundred-item kill-list between the lot of us. *No.* Our enemies will chemically-burn into radioactive puddles by themselves. You.” 

She spun. Pointed in Saint’s direction. 

“First off, enjoy yourself. You need it. But don’t drink too much. I know that right now, you feel invincible, but look at yourself. Emaciated as fuck.”

Saint hardly heard. Once wasted and blood-choked, her lungs felt powerful and clean. 

And still, she could hardly draw breath. 

The ones she held most dearly on earth, the ones she thought she’d never see again, the ones who had haunted her nightmares and dreams for nights and nights and nights until she’d lost count, too weak to snick them off on the wall.

Laughing. Skinny-dipping. 

Under a full-fucking moon, like a pack of goddamn werewolves.

Free. 

“But…you died. I saw you die.”

“Well, yeah. I mean. We did die.” Lightbulb shrugged. “Canary says Hekate brought us back. Goddess of the crossroads and the unquiet dead.” 

As a fresh gale of screaming laughter floated into the sky, she flashed Saint a grin. “I’d say we’re pretty unquiet, wouldn’t you?”

stories.title: Frenzy
stories.description: A man-eating siren learns to set boundaries.
stories.content:
Her hair needs a trim. Saltwater curls the ends, hanging in loose tangles around her face. Although she almost never wears makeup, she wears it tonight. Neon eyeliner, silvery-white mascara, plum lipstick. Beneath her suit jacket, the vest grips her as tightly as a corset. 

The first time she spots him looking, she almost feels—interested. 

Not *interested*, interested. More like….

Huh. Noted.

The second time he looks, annoyance prickles. 

The third time, she stares back. Holds his gaze and waits, triumphant when he breaks first. 

Calloway is not, by nature, a confrontational person. Soft-spoken, prone to anxiety.

So. She is proud of herself. 

But then he looks again, and Calloway begins to feel like prey. 

“Jesus, fuck,” she whispers, laughing a little.

Beside her, Poppy frowns. 

They sit with their bodies aligned. Shoulders, hips, touchingly close. Their shoes, braced together while the boat dips and swoops over the waves outside.

Poppy wears a suit, too, but thinner and almost flowy-looking, no structure in it. Nothing underneath but a wisp of lace for a bra. Her cropped hair falls piratically over one eye. 

“That guy. He won’t stop looking, it’s….”

Calloway bites her lip. Notes the divot between Poppy’s brows, the glint in her seaglass eyes. 

“Should I say something, maybe?”  

Amusement dances on Poppy’s smile. “No,” she murmurs. “Don’t say anything.” Bringing Calloway’s hand to her lips, she kisses the knuckles, and Calloway melts, pulse beating like a bird’s wings inside her wrists. Dazzled, as always, by Poppy’s….

Well. By Poppy. 

Poppy rises. For a splintering instant, Calloway fears her girlfriend will confront the man herself—but she passes him by on her way to the center of the cocktail bar. The rocking on the water casts an additional sway to her hips as she mounts the stage, empty, save for a piano. She sits, fingers fluttering over the keys.

And then, to Calloway’s astonishment, she begins to sing. 

And it’s like—

It’s like—

Calloway doesn’t exactly know. 

But everything changes. Everything. The room, bombilating with people, falls silent. Poppy holds them spellbound.

Calloway, no exception. 

Her mind fogs and glitters. Time begins to loop and fray, to lose meaning. Desire tumbles through her. A giddy, playful thing.

When Poppy stops playing, it does not break the spell. Nobody moves, no one speaks as she prowls from the stage, retracing her steps. When she reaches the man who had stared, she touches his arm, urging him to his feet. 

They kiss.

Something rattles in Calloway’s head. Quietly, darkly, as though from very far away. Inside a cage, bottom of a well. Rattles harder, as Poppy walks past Calloway like they’ve never met. The man follows closely behind, docile as a child, and the pair leaves together. 

That, at last, breaks the spell. 

Calloway stares into her drink, puzzled, as the bar jolts into noise and movement around her. 

She doesn’t know how long Poppy sang up there, but she thinks that perhaps, if Poppy had done it long enough, Calloway might have died of thirst. 

Holding this drink. Listening to Poppy sing.

Calloway hadn’t known Poppy *could* sing. 

And…

And Poppy….

Poppy had kissed someone else. 

In the bottom of the well, the cage opens. A monster claws its way up, a roughshod amalgamation of her pain. Humiliation and rage and shock and wet, sticky hurt.

Calloway’s hands spasm into fists, a reflex. The glass in her hand breaks, pieces sliding neatly into the meat of her palm. Blood spills into her lap, but Calloway can’t feel it. 

Poppy left with him. Left Calloway. Sitting here, alone, idiotic, and Calloway hadn’t…hadn’t *done* anything, she hadn’t….

She would love to march onto that stage right now and flip that fucking piano. But her body is beginning to tremble, and….

And people are staring. 

She rushes from the bar, stumbles down the stairs, down, down, down, blood peppering the carpet beneath her as she streaks down the hall of her floor.

Outside, the ocean grows volatile. 

She doesn’t understand how Poppy could have done this. How someone who felt safe could change in a blink, no warning signs. Calloway is vigilant about those, or she thinks she is. Tries to be. 

She hadn’t found any. Hadn’t seen, and now….

Finally, she makes it to the room. Panic flares when it doesn’t work the first time, but the light blinks green on the second, and she throws herself inside. 

Her knees hit the bathroom tile. Hot tears spill. 

She’d never wanted to fall in love again, but with Poppy, it didn’t feel like falling. Felt like…the opposite. Like *not* falling, walking on a tightrope. Holding Poppy’s hand for balance, wobbly and frightened, knowing the deadly splat that awaited her should Poppy choose to let go.

She’d let go now.

You love someone, you don’t render them a sniveling mess to be scraped off the bathroom floor. You break up with them before you cheat on them, and you certainly don’t cheat in the middle of the ocean, forcing them to huddle alone in the room you share together, counting, excruciatingly aware of every minute you spend touching someone else.

Her stomach wrenches. The kiss was—horrible. 

The waiting is worse. The waiting is torture. 

Breathing hurts, and she begins to hyperventilate. The bathroom shrinks around her, so she staggers to her feet. Exits, crossing the bedroom to the balcony, where, gasping, she bursts outside. Her body doubles over the railing, clinging as she gulps for air. 

Salt hits her face. Dark waves rise massively over her. Towering, the size of buildings. 

She is absolutely, definitely not supposed to be outside. 

 There must have been—an announcement. A warning.

 In her misery, she must not have heard.

The ship casts spots of light over the water. Near the foaming surface, Calloway sees a flash of—something. 

Scales. Webbed, blueish fingers. 

Crimson clouds, ballooning and swirling.

An old, intelligent dread slips inside her. 

Turning, her hands slide impotently over the knob. For a terrifying instant, she thinks she is locked out, then she realises her feet have slid from beneath her; she has sagged against the door. 

When she steps back, she slips. Her back slams into the railing; her arms pinwheel.

She recovers. Barely. 

Her stomach climbs into her throat as she wrenches open the door, falling inside and scrambling to the toilet to be sick again. 

…

In the tub, Calloway wakes to the press of Poppy’s lips on hers. 

“Hey, you,” Poppy murmurs. Wariness darts in her eyes. Her lips hover inches from Calloway’s, hands resting on her cheeks. 

“How are you feeling?” she asks, watching Calloway carefully. 

Calloway squirms. Considering.  “I feel….”

Her mouth tastes like something died in it, and her hand hurts. Muscles knot in her neck from where she’d tried to cuddle a spigot.

“Weird,” she decides.

Poppy warms, but only a little. 

Curiously, Calloway blinks at her. “What are you wearing under that robe?” 

“Hmm?” Poppy looks down. She is no longer wearing her suit. “Oh. Nothing.” 

Calloway giggles. 

Poppy’s answering grin gets a hitch in it, one side lifting higher than the other. Then her features draw suddenly tight. 

“You’re bleeding.”

“I bandaged it,” Calloway explains, holding up her hand. Toilet paper and pimple patches. 

 Rising, Poppy shakes her head. “We can do better than that. Head out in a few minutes, find the ship’s physician.” After a moment, she strides from the doorway, leaving Calloway to luxuriate alone. 

She loves this, this feeling. Washing through her, injected into her veins. 

Eventually, she remembers what to do with her limbs.

In the bedroom, Poppy opens the balcony doors again. The weather has calmed somewhat; the waves tossing the boat not quite so violently as before, but water drenches the carpet. 

Calloway sinks onto the bed, throws out an imperious arm. “Come here.”

Poppy complies. Braces her knees on either side of Calloway’s lap, straddling her. Calloway dips her fingers beneath the hem of Poppy’s robe, pleased at how Poppy’s breathing changes, the stippling of goosebumps on Poppy’s thighs where she touches her. 

But when Calloway moves for a kiss Poppy holds herself back.

“Contact toxins,” she mumbles, expression shuttering. “I’m still…giving it off, and you shouldn’t take too much. We need to be careful.” Her tongue presses against the inside of her cheek, and she blinks, grimacing, as though discovering something unwelcome between her teeth.

When she speaks again, her voice is low. Hard. 

“We’re not doing this again, understand? I’ll take you anywhere you want on land, but no ships. No beaches. I…tried, okay, and I can’t. Hard limit.” 

Calloway melts onto her back. “I don’t know what any of that means,” she complains.

Poppy doesn’t reply. Easing off the mattress, she returns to the balcony. Leans against the open doorway, giving Calloway her profile as she watches the sea.

Calloway’s head lolls to the side. “I saw a monster in the water,” she says quietly. “I think.”

“Yeah?”

“It was really scary.”

At that, Poppy huffs out a laugh. Calloway finds herself arching her back a little, hoping Poppy will come back. 

She doesn’t.

But her eyes are hot as coals, her expression disarmingly tender. “Don’t be scared. What you saw isn’t out there anymore.”

Her voice cracks into the room. Hoarse. Nothing musical or beguiling about it.
